input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names. In .NET 11 (which would be .NET 9's successor - actually .NET 11 would be released in 2026), System.Text.Json should have a built-in `PascalCase` naming policy.

Let me think about this. .NET versioning: .NET 5 (2020), .NET 6 (2021), .NET 7 (2022), .NET 8 (2023), .NET 9 (2024), .NET 10 (2025), .NET 11 (2026).


Since we're in May 2026, .NET 11 might be in preview or recently released. System.Text.Json has `CamelCase` since .NET Core 3.0, and .NET 8 added snake_case and kebab-case variants, but PascalCase is actually the default behavior—System.Text.Json preserves property names as-is without transformation.

The real issue here is that the record's positional parameters (`name`, `age`) become properties with identical casing, so they serialize as lowercase in JSON. The user needs PascalCase output (`Name`, `Age`), which means I need to check if `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` exists or if there's another way to achieve this conversion. I need to verify whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` actually exists in any released .NET version, since I'm not confident about this. Let me just create a test project and try it out—if it doesn't work, I'll find the correct approach for converting those lowercase property names to PascalCase in the JSON output.

data.content: .NET 11 preview SDK is available. Let me create the project and try `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase`:
